1 
 Title 
 
Deciphering the Functional Immune Composition and Its Clinical Impact in a Cohort of HGSC 
HR- Proficient Interval Debulking Samples 
Supervisors 
 
Supervisor 1: Anniina Färkkilä, MD PhD 
Principal investigator in Färkkilä lab, Research Program in Systems Oncology (ONCOSYS), 
Faculty of Medicine, University of Helsinki. 
Supervisor 2: Ada Junquera Mencía, MSc 
Doctoral researcher in Färkkilä lab, Research Program in Systems Oncology (ONCOSYS), 
Faculty of Medicine, University of Helsinki. 
Unit 
 
Research Program in Systems Oncology (ONCOSYS), Faculty of Medicine, University of 
Helsinki 
Scientific background 
 
Ovarian cancer is one of the most prevalent gynecological cancers, known for its poor prognosis 
and high mortality rate (Momenimovahed et al., 2019) . This disease is highly heterogeneous 
and includes several histological subtypes, with high -grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) being 
the most lethal, responsible for 70 -80% of ovarian cancer deaths (Bowtell et al., 2015) . Its high 
mortality rate is primarily due to the lack of effective treatment options and the delayed 
diagnosis , often influenced by the late onset of symptoms, the tumor's asymptomatic growth, 
and insufficient screening methods (Momenimovahed et al., 2019) . 
The standard treatment for HGSC is primary debulking surgery (PDS) followed by adjuvant 
chemotherapy.

c growth, 
and insufficient screening methods (Momenimovahed et al., 2019) . 
The standard treatment for HGSC is primary debulking surgery (PDS) followed by adjuvant 
chemotherapy. For patients with advanced stages of the disease who are not suitable candidates 
for PDS, the preferred option is neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) followed by interval 
debulking surgery (IDS) and adjuvant chemotherapy . (Armstrong et al., 2021) Although 
patients initially respond well to chemotherapy, the majority eventually develop resistance to 
the platinum -based chemotherapy (Böhm et al., 2016) .

2 
 Targeted therapies have emerged in this context as the main breakthrough within ovarian cancer 
treatment. One of the most successful has been poly (ADP -ribose) polymerase (PARP) 
inhibitors , which suppress DNA repair in cancer cells and have proven eff ective in tumors with 
pre-existing defects in the homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair pathway (Bowtell et 
al., 2015) . However, patients with HR -proficient tumors do not benefit from PARP inhibitors . 
In such cases, the primary g oal of treatment is often to manage symptoms and relieve pain to 
improve quality of life . There fore, there is a critical need to further analyze the TME in HR -
proficient tumors to better understand its characteristics and role in disease progression, 
potentially offering new treatment options for this patient subgroup . (Stiegeler et al., 2024) 
Understanding the tumor microenvironment (TME ) offers promising new targets for treating 
HGSC and potentially improving existing therapies. The TME is a complex network in which 
tumor cells interact with other cell types, such as fibroblasts, endothelial cells, and metabolites. 
The TME plays a crucial role in the development of the primary tumor and the formation of 
metastases. (Jiang et al., 2020) Immune cells within the TME have their own specific relevance, 
contributing to both immune activation and immunosuppression .

nd the formation of 
metastases. (Jiang et al., 2020) Immune cells within the TME have their own specific relevance, 
contributing to both immune activation and immunosuppression . Tumor -infiltrating 
lymphocytes (TILs) , includ ing both T cells and B cell s, have been associated with longer 
progression -free survival (PFS) (Rodriguez et al., 2018) . In contrast , CD4+ regulatory T cells 
(Tregs) and tumor -associated macrophages (TAMs) of the M2 phenotype exhibit tumor -
promoting activity and immunosuppressive functions . The composition of immune cells within 
the TME is strongly correlated with treatment responses and overall survival . (Hao et al., 2020) 
Despite the promise of immunotherapies, they have largely failed to achieve significant results 
in ovarian cancer, partly due to the complex immunosuppressive landscape of the tumor 
immune microenvironment (TIME) (Rodriguez et al., 2018) . Moreover , NACT treatment has 
been shown to significantly influence both the immunostimulatory and immunosuppressive 
functions of the TIME, affecting both the quantity and activity of immune cells (Xue et al., 
2023) . 
Further exploration of NACT’s effects on immune cells and their functional markers , especially 
in HR proficient patient with limited treatment options , along with the integration of TME

ploration of NACT’s effects on immune cells and their functional markers , especially 
in HR proficient patient with limited treatment options , along with the integration of TME 
analysis with genomic and molecular testing , is crucial (Jiang et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2023) .

3 
 Aims and objectives 
 
Main aim 
 
The primary objective of this master’s thesis is to investigate the tumor immune 
microenvironment (TIME) in homologous re combination (HR) proficient HGSC IDS samples 
using existing data from the ongoing Oncosys -OVA clinical trial . This analysis aims to provide 
insight s into TIME’s composition after chemotherapy and its impact on the clinical outcomes . 
Sub aims 
 
1. Refining image -derived quantified data through data normalization and assignment of 
precise phenotype labels to the cells of the TME (accounting for cell type specific 
marker intensity), also identifying the most important functional states (determined by 
Ki67, pStat1, TIM3, FOXP3, PD -1). 
2. Exploring the composition of the tumor immune microenvironment by analyzing the 
immune functional states, their relative abundance across the cohort , as well as assessing 
the cellular diversity and the possible correlations with in the cell types . 
3. Identify patient subgroups with similar immune profiles based on the samples’ 
composition analysis and study the relationship between clinical factors (PFS, PFI, 
CRS) and the distinct immune cell types and the functional states. 
Materials & methods 
 
Materials 
 
The data used in this thesis was collected from newly diagnosed patients in the Oncosys -OVA 
clinical trial.

types and the functional states. 
Materials & methods 
 
Materials 
 
The data used in this thesis was collected from newly diagnosed patients in the Oncosys -OVA 
clinical trial. 28 IDS samples were selected based on criteria such as progression -free survival 
of more than six months and a minimum tumor purity of 10 -15% according to an expert 
pathologist . Surgical and clinical data were gathered, and BRCA1/2 mutational profiling along 
with genomic scarring (ovaHRDscar) was conducted . This data enables a comprehensive 
assessment of chemotherapy responses, genomic status, and disease progression . 
From the same cohort , FFPE samples were collected and analyzed using tissue cyclic 
immunofluorescence (tCycIF) , a highly multiplexed immun ofluorescence imaging technique . 
In this antibody -based method, 3 -4 antibodies are added in each cycle and imaged with a highly 
reliable RareCyte CyteFinder II. Each sample underwent multiple imaging cycles with a panel 
comprising 36 different markers to characteriz e cell populations in the TME, their functional

4 
 states, cell cycle sta ges and DNA damage patterns . The image dataset to be utilized has already 
been processed, segmented and quantified. 
Methods 
 
This thesis is entirely a bioinformatics project, with packages in Python and R employed 
throughout its full extent. 
Normalization of data 
Normalization using state-of-the-art methods will be optimized in R . 
Cell phenotyping with scimap 
Utilizing specific bioimage analysis software called scimap in combination with Napari image -
viewer , cell type calling is performed on the images through the establishment of thresholds 
and the creation of logic tables to assign the cell labels. Phenotyp es are then identified by 
analyzing the levels of the following markers : PanCK, PAX8, CD45, CD4, CD8 a, CD20, IBA1, 
CD163, CD11c, NKG2a, Vimentin, aSMA, FAP and CD31 . 
Data analysis 
Data analysis and visualiz ation will be performed using various packages in R and Python, such 
as complexHeatmap, seaborn and pheatmap . The composition analysis will be performed using 
different types of plots such as, bar plots, heatmaps , UMAPs and box plots . The clinical data 
will then be correlated to the different subgroups of immune cells and the results from the 
composition analysis . 
Availability of required special equipment and materials

will then be correlated to the different subgroups of immune cells and the results from the

Availability of required special equipment and materials 
 
All computational infrastructure required for this thesis will be provided by Färkkilä lab. No 
biological specimens are directly handled by the student and the clinical data will be stored 
under the highest security standards as specified in the section below. 
Ethical considerations 
 
All data used in this thesis originate s from the prospective Oncosys -Ova study and has been 
approved by the ethics committees of the Women’s Clinic, Helsinki University Hospital (HUH) 
and Helsinki Biobank .

5 
 Patient IDs and other personal information in the clinical data will be handled with the highest 
security and confidentiality on the HUH server, using the HUH Datalake at the Helsinki 
Biobank. Access to clinical data is strictly restricted to authorized personnel for retrieval and 
use. In publicly available data, each patient will be assigned a unique, randomized ID to prevent 
any risk of patient identification. 
Timetable 
 
This project will be carried out between August 2024 and March 2025 (Figure 1) and is divided 
into three aims, detailed in the sub -aims section. I will work part-time (80%) througho ut the 
thesis work . The project will start in August 2024 with Aim 1, which focuses on data 
refinement . Aim 2, involving composition analysis of the TME , is planned for September and 
October . Aim 3, investigating correlations between the composition analysis and clinical data, 
is expected to start in Novemb er and be completed by January . The thesis writing is planned 
for two months , from January to March 2025 . 
 
Figure 1. Timeline for master's thesis project. Figure created in Canva. 
Supervision 
 
I will receive ongoing guidance and feedback from my supervisors throughout the project. 
Regular meetings will be held to review progress, discuss results, and adjust the project's 
direction as needed to achieve the research goals.
